paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1184 English Language — AI-authored Simplified Practice 17: The Last Runner (§B style, simplified band)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Sec 1 boy who is asked at the last minute to run the fourth leg of the inter-class relay, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [15 marks] — AI-authored simplified practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1184/02 (formerly 1128) Section B format and calibrated to the SEAB assessment objectives (literal / inferential comprehension, vocabulary in context, language for effect), pitched at students stretching from middle secondary toward the full O-Level standard. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. Items mix mark values [1]/[2]: 7 short-answer questions (Q1-Q7) plus a 4-blank emotion-flowchart MCQ (Q8). Answer in your own words as far as possible.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
My name is Aaron. I am in Sec 1E. I have never been good at sports. In Primary 6, I always finished near the back during the 2.4km run. In Sec 1, I joined the Library Club, not any sport.

Paragraph 2
On Wednesday afternoon, our PE teacher Mr Lee called me to the staffroom. He told me that two boys in my class were down with fever and one had gone on an overseas school trip. The inter-class relay was on Friday. He needed a fourth runner. He looked at me kindly and asked if I would try.

Paragraph 3
I wanted to say no. I knew I would lose the race for 1E. But Mr Lee did not push me. He simply waited. In the end I nodded, because I did not know how to refuse him politely.

Paragraph 4
On Thursday evening, I could not sleep. I lay in bed in our HDB flat in Yishun and stared at the ceiling fan. I thought about my classmates' faces if we came last. At three in the morning I was still awake, listening to the MRT trains starting up.

Paragraph 5
On Friday at the school field, my hands were cold even though the sun was already hot. The first three runners from 1E did their best. When the baton came to me, we were in fourth place. I gripped the baton so tightly that my fingers hurt.

Paragraph 6
And then I ran. I do not remember thinking about anything. I only remember the sound of my own breathing, very loud in my ears, and the white line on the track moving past my feet. Halfway down the home straight, I overtook the boy from 1F. Near the finish line, I passed the boy from 1D as well. We came third out of seven classes.

Paragraph 7
At the end of the race, I bent over with my hands on my knees, trying to breathe. Mr Lee walked over. He did not say 'well done' or 'good job'. He said one sentence: 'Effort is also a result.' Then he patted me on the shoulder and walked away.

Paragraph 8
On the bus home that afternoon, I kept thinking about what Mr Lee had said. I had heard the same sentence many times before, on posters and in assemblies. But for the first time, I understood what it actually meant. I had not won. I had not even come second. But I had done something that I, last Wednesday, would have said was impossible. That, too, was a result.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1184 §B style practice, simplified band; not from a past examination paper. HDB: Singapore public housing flats; PE: Physical Education; inter-class relay: a Singapore school event.)

questions.stem: Q1. From Paragraph 2, what two reasons did Mr Lee give for needing a fourth runner? [1]
questions.answer: two boys had fever and one was overseas

questions.stem: Q2. What does the word 'kindly' (Paragraph 2) suggest about the way Mr Lee asked Aaron? [1]
questions.answer: gently and warmly, not forcing him

questions.stem: Q3. What does the phrase 'I did not know how to refuse him politely' (Paragraph 3) suggest about why Aaron said yes? [1]
questions.answer: he agreed only out of politeness

questions.stem: Q4. Using your own words, explain why Aaron could not sleep on Thursday night. (Paragraph 4) [2]
questions.answer: ① he was anxious about the race the next day; ② he worried his classmates would blame him if 1E came last

questions.stem: Q5. Using your own words, explain why Mr Lee said 'Effort is also a result' instead of 'well done'. (Paragraph 7) [2]
questions.answer: ① he wanted Aaron to value trying hard, not only winning; ② a simple 'well done' would not have taught him that lesson

questions.stem: Q6. Using your own words, explain what Aaron finally understood about himself in Paragraph 8. [2]
questions.answer: ① he could do more than he had believed possible; ② trying hard at something difficult is itself a real result, not just winning

questions.stem: Q7. What is the effect of 'I gripped the baton so tightly that my fingers hurt' (Paragraph 5)? [2]
questions.answer: the physical detail shows how tense and nervous Aaron was — the pain in his fingers makes the pressure he felt before running vivid

sections.instruction: Q8. Aaron's dominant feeling shifts as the days leading up to and during the relay pass. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes his dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: reluctant / proud / focused / suspicious / relieved / annoyed / encouraged / sleepy. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Last Runner' narrative in Exercise 1 above. Aaron's feelings shift over the course of the story — from being reluctant when Mr Lee asks him to run the fourth leg of the relay, to being deeply focused during the race itself, to a wave of relief when he realises that 1E has come third out of seven, ending in being encouraged on the bus home when he finally understands what Mr Lee meant by 'effort is also a result'. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q8(i). What best describes Aaron's dominant feeling in Paragraph 3, as he stands in the staffroom and finally nods at Mr Lee because he does not know how to refuse him politely? [1]
Q8(ii). What best describes Aaron's dominant feeling in Paragraph 6, as he runs down the track hearing only his own breathing and watching the white line move past his feet? [1]
Q8(iii). What best describes Aaron's dominant feeling at the very end of Paragraph 6, when he realises that 1E has come third out of seven classes? [1]
Q8(iv). What best describes Aaron's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8, on the bus home as he understands for the first time what Mr Lee meant by 'effort is also a result'? [1]
